summary: Armin argues that most agentic-coding workflow automations he tried didn’t stick, not because the ideas are bad, but because they didn’t outperform simply talking to the model with rich context. He frames automation with strict rules: only automate frequent tasks and delete automations that don’t get used. Slash commands, hooks, print mode, and sub-agents all fell short due to friction, lack of structure, poor reliability, or context chaos. He finds that copy/paste, speech-to-text, and ad‑hoc guidance are more effective than rigid prompts. The post concludes that context management and reliability are still the bottlenecks, and over-automation risks mental disengagement and quality regressions.
key_points: Automation only pays off for repeated tasks; unused automations should be deleted to avoid clutter.
Slash commands suffer from unstructured arguments and missing autocomplete, making them less useful than expected.
Hooks and print mode promise determinism but are unreliable, slow, or poorly integrated with execution flow.
Sub-agents and parallel tasks often create chaos when reads/writes mix; fresh sessions or simple notes work better.
Over-reliance on agents can reduce engineering judgment and increase regressions.
money_quotes: Non-working automations turn out to be quite common.
So I end up doing the simplest thing possible most of the time: just talk to the machine more, give it more context, keep the audio input going, and dump my train of thought into the prompt.
Using an LLM for tasks that don't require inference is the wrong approach in my book.
Context engineering and management remain major challenges.
Tasks that don't parallelize well — especially those mixing reads and writes — create chaos.
There is a big hidden risk with automation through LLMs: it encourages mental disengagement.
key_insight: Agentic workflows only help when they beat direct, high-context communication, and today the reliability and context-management gaps often make automation a net loss.
